coredata.dc:title: On the characterization of lithium-ion batteries under overtemperature and overcharge conditions: Identification of abuse areas and experimental validation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Lalinde, Iñaki
Berrueta, Alberto
Arza, Joseba
Sanchis, Pablo
Ursúa, Alfredo
coredata.dc:description: Although lithium-ion batteries have gained considerable popularity in renewable energy and electric vehicle applications, their safety still remains a concern under certain voltage, temperature, or state of charge conditions. This can lead to degradation and potential thermal runaway. In order to improve the safety assessment of LIBs based on their operating conditions, it is therefore essential to analyze not only their safe operating area but also their abuse region. This study focuses on the characterization of the abuse region of lithium-ion batteries by proposing a new methodology in which four areas of abuse are identified and experimentally validated using a commercial 3.6 Ah pouch cell. The cell is subjected to overtemperature and overcharge conditions, exploring various states of charge (0 to 200%) and ambient temperatures (25 to 100 °C). The influence of temperature and state of charge on the battery’s behavior is thoroughly analyzed to fully characterize the abuse region. Results reveal the limiting temperatures and states of charge that define the boundaries of the abuse areas. By extending the characterization of LIBs behavior beyond the safe operation area with the determination of four areas of abuse, this article contributes to a better understanding of the phenomena and abuse mechanisms produced by overtemperature and overcharge events with an eye to improving battery safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Thermal runaway
Battery safety
Abuse area
Abuse testing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: General outline of the phenomena occurring in a LIB according to temperature and SOC ranges and grouped by the areas of abuse defined in this work. (a) Phenomena due to overtemperature. (b) Phenomena due to overcharge.

Abuse testing procedure. (a) Overtemperature method in test 4. (b) Measurement of
R
D C
in overtemperature tests. (a) Overcharge method in test 10.

Abuse area limiting temperatures obtained from overtemperature results.

R
D C
measurements in per unit, in relation to the value at 25 °C, at different SOCs vs. cell temperature during overtemperature tests.

Abuse area limit SOCs and temperatures obtained from overcharge tests depending on the ambient temperature (
T
a m b
) during the abuse: (a) SOCs vs.
T
a m b
. (b) Temperatures vs.
T
a m b
.

T
a m b
.

T
a m b
= 25 °C.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
*
2
T
s a f e , m a x
does not exist in overcharge conditions.

*
3
The onset of degradation occurs previously during overcharging.

*
4
Venting occurs during overcharging prior to the overtemperature test.

*
1
The maximum safe conditions for SOC and temperature are reached before the experiment, during sample preparation (
T
s a f e , m a x
= 45 °C).

*
2
The onset of degradation before the experiment, during sample preparation.

head.ce:title: On the characterization of lithium-ion batteries under overtemperature and overcharge conditions: Identification of abuse areas and experimental validation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although lithium-ion batteries have gained considerable popularity in renewable energy and electric vehicle applications, their safety still remains a concern under certain voltage, temperature, or state of charge conditions. This can lead to degradation and potential thermal runaway. In order to improve the safety assessment of LIBs based on their operating conditions, it is therefore essential to analyze not only their safe operating area but also their abuse region. This study focuses on the characterization of the abuse region of lithium-ion batteries by proposing a new methodology in which four areas of abuse are identified and experimentally validated using a commercial 3.6 Ah pouch cell. The cell is subjected to overtemperature and overcharge conditions, exploring various states of charge (0 to 200%) and ambient temperatures (25 to 100 °C). The influence of temperature and state of charge on the battery’s behavior is thoroughly analyzed to fully characterize the abuse region. Results reveal the limiting temperatures and states of charge that define the boundaries of the abuse areas. By extending the characterization of LIBs behavior beyond the safe operation area with the determination of four areas of abuse, this article contributes to a better understanding of the phenomena and abuse mechanisms produced by overtemperature and overcharge events with an eye to improving battery safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are widely used in portable electronic devices due to their advantages, such as high energy and power density, long cycle life and low self-discharge. The need to integrate renewable energy and electric vehicles has further driven their development, making them the leading technology compared to other energy storage systems [1]. However, safety issues relating to LIBs are a serious concern that needs to be addressed. In particular, those resulting from thermal runaway (TR), which is the worst-case scenario that a LIB can face and leads to its self-destruction [2]. TR poses a risk to property and people alike, given that flammable and toxic gases are emitted and high temperatures are reached, with explosions and fires being a likely consequence [3].

LIBs represent an electrochemical system consisting of different components, whose materials are selected in order to enhance their electrochemical performance. As a result, the safety level of the materials used in LIBs is sometimes disregarded, making them more vulnerable. Indeed, this is a current problem because in the search for higher energy densities, unstable materials are being used, which reduce the level of safety [4]. Each manufacturer provides the safety conditions under which it can guarantee the normal operation of its batteries, such as temperature and voltage ranges [5] or maximum charge–discharge currents depending on temperature [6], which is how the safe operating area (SOA) is defined. A number of international standards and regulations have been established for the safety testing of LIBs under various abuse conditions [7]. However, in general, a pass/fail criterion is considered and little information is available on what happens to LIBs if safety limits are exceeded. This information would be extremely valuable in designing preventive measures that can manage situations in which unsafe conditions occur. For this reason and because of the wide variety of types of LIBs, the behavior of LIBs outside safe conditions needs further investigation.

Characterizing the SOA of LIBs involves conducting extensive experiments to determine the behavior and performance under various operating conditions. This characterization includes evaluating parameters such as voltage, current, temperature, capacity, and impedance within the defined SOA [8,9]. The monitoring and control by Battery Management Systems (BMS) coordinated with other active or passive devices have reported suitable results in order to guarantee the safe conditions of LIBs [10]. However, unsafe conditions are unavoidable and may be caused by a faulty design or abuses that take the battery out of the safe region. The main types of abuse can be classified as mechanical (impact, deformation, perforation, etc.), thermal (high or low temperatures) and electrical (overcharge, overdischarge, short circuits, etc.) [11]. Although mechanical abuse becomes important in electric vehicles, it is of less concern in other applications. Thermal abuse on the other hand is of great interest as cell materials are very sensitive to high temperatures, which may affect their properties and stability. The same applies to electrical abuse, as BMSs can fail and component stability may also be threatened. Overcharge is considered to be the most dangerous type of abuse test given that more energy is stored inside the battery and will be released during a failure event. For this study the authors have focused on overtemperature and overcharge abuses, because these are considered to be the most dangerous and likely in real applications.

Overtemperature and overcharge abuse testing is a recurring topic in the literature related to LIBs safety and thermal runaway. A number of researchers have identified the main reactions that originate in the cells when the temperature or the state of charge (SOC) exceeds safe limits [12–14]. Given that the mechanisms of each type of abuse differ, it is common to analyze the reactions and processes that take place in each one separately. These processes mainly depend on the materials of the cells (chemistry) [15], the conditions of abuse (heating method, ambient temperature, charge current) [16] and the state of the battery (SOC, SOH) [17]. Ideally, it would be advisable to standardize the safety assessment of LIBs regardless of the type of abuse or chemistry. Therefore, in order to assess the safety performance of LIBs, three characteristic temperatures are generally considered (
T
1
,
T
2
and
T
3
) [18], which represent the onset temperature of abnormal heat generation, the triggering temperature of TR and the maximum temperature reached during the TR respectively. However, these three temperatures would still not consider the venting process, which determines the moment when gases are released outside due to increased pressure inside the cell. Considering this phenomenon is crucial because this release of gases represents another safety feature of a battery and would be situated between
T
1
and
T
2
processes. It is therefore the reason why venting of LIBs is being examined to assess safety [19]. By considering these temperatures, it is possible to define the different stages that occur as a LIB undergoes abuse leading to a TR, as has been done in various studies [20,21]. Finally, it is important to emphasize that these temperatures that define the onset of key processes in the behavior of a LIB under abuse conditions are significantly affected by the SOC. Therefore, it would be more appropriate to assess each phenomenon not only with temperature but also with SOC.

Taking all the above into account, this article presents a new methodology for the characterization of the abuse region in LIBs under overtemperature and overcharge conditions. This methodology is based on the identification of four abuse areas that extend the concept of the SOA to abusive conditions. The areas are characterized in two dimensions being limited by boundaries defined by corresponding temperature and SOC. The proposed boundaries of these abuse identify key phenomena and processes that lead a LIB TR. These phenomena manifest in any type of LIB, regardless of its chemistry, size, or shape, making this characterization methodology applicable to all types of LIBs. The description of each area is provided in this article, taking into account the mechanisms of the two types of abuse analyzed. Furthermore, various methods for determining the boundaries between areas are presented. This characterization methodology is experimentally validated using a commercial cell subjected to nine overtemperature tests at different SOCs and four overcharge tests at different ambient temperatures. Finally, based on the test results, the characterization of the overtemperature and overcharge abuse region is presented, identifying the extent of each area as a function of temperature and SOC.

This paper is organized as follows. Section 2 describes the mechanism of the two types of abuse investigated. Section 3 defines the abuse areas and proposes how to identify them. Section 4 describes the investigated cell, the tests conducted and the equipment used. Section 5 analyzes the results of the abuse tests and discusses the characterization of the abuse region based on the abuse areas. Finally, Section 6 draws the main conclusions.

In general, the maximum temperature at which a LIB can operate safely (
T
s a f e , m a x
) is around 60 °C. However, manufacturers often recommend lower temperatures to improve performance and extend lifetime. Even if the cell temperature exceeds
T
s a f e , m a x
, no significant degradation reactions occur, thanks to the safety margin that ensures the safe operation of the cell. As the temperature of the cell increases, its internal resistance decreases, due to the reduced viscosity of the electrolyte which improves the conductivity of the lithium ions [22].

However, reactions are triggered when the temperature continues to increase. The temperature at which degradation starts is noted
T
d e g
and is followed by heat and gas generation. The decomposition of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) is known to be the first exothermic reaction to take place. Depending on the composition of the SEI layer, the onset temperature of this reaction can be between 80 to 120 °C [23]. The decomposition of the SEI exposes the lithium intercalated in the anode, which rapidly reacts with the organic solvent of the electrolyte [12]. From the reaction between the intercalated lithium and the electrolyte, a second SEI layer may be formed, which again decomposes as a result of the above-mentioned reactions. The decomposition and regeneration of the SEI occur simultaneously within the temperature range of 120–250 °C [13]. In addition, the continued increase in the cell temperature leads to the vaporization of the organic electrolyte components. The organic solvents making up the electrolyte, such as dimethyl carbonate (DMC), ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC), diethyl carbonate (DEC) and ethylene carbonate (EC), have boiling points of 91, 107, 127 and 244 °C, respectively.

As a result of these reactions, gases accumulate inside the cell, increasing the internal pressure and causing it to swell. When the pressure reaches the threshold, the cell opens and the gases escape to the outside.
T
v e n t
is the temperature at which venting occurs.

Polyethylene (PE) and polypropylene (PP) are the most widely used separator materials in LIBs, with melting points of 130 and 165 °C, respectively. If the temperature of the cell reaches these values and the separator melts, then it can no longer prevent the electrical contact between electrodes and an internal short circuit (ISC) may be triggered. This can produce a sudden drop in the cell voltage and more heat is generated.

At higher temperatures, exothermic reactions occur at the cathode leading to its decomposition [13]. Furthermore, a reaction takes place at the interface between the cathode and the electrolyte [18]. Electrolyte decomposition also occurs at high temperatures, which depends on the availability of electrolyte as it is consumed in the above reactions. All these reactions generate an enormous amount of heat and are responsible for triggering TR. In order to estimate the onset temperature of TR,
T
0 , T R
is defined as the temperature at which the temperature rate exceeds 10 °C/min. During the TR process, the cell reaches a maximum temperature (
T
m a x
), which depends on a number of factors such as LIB chemistry, energy density, SOC or SOH.

When the cell is overcharged above 100% SOC, the cell is charged normally, as the electrodes are oversized, providing a safety margin [24]. Therefore, even if the voltage rises above the cut-off value no abnormal temperature increase is observed and a normal appearance is maintained.

However, continuous overcharging causes the delithiation of the cathode and lithiation of the anode beyond the safe operation limits of the electrode structures. Due to the excessive lithium intercalation, lithium plating occurs at the surface of the anode [13]. The metallic lithium reacts with the SEI layer and the electrolyte, resulting in increased heat generation [25]. In addition, the temperature increases more significantly as a result of electrolyte decomposition and oxidation [24], and the voltage of the cell increases faster, due to the negative shift caused by lithium plating on the anode [26].

If the cell temperature reaches the onset temperature of the decomposition of the SEI layer, the decomposition and regeneration reactions occur simultaneously. The increasing rate of voltage slows down and even stops or becomes negative, producing a voltage plateau as a result of lithium consumption in the above reactions [26]. As a result of these reactions, gases are generated inside the cell. This causes it to swell and increases the distance between electrodes, which then increases the internal resistance.

A rise in temperature may trigger the reactions identified in the overtemperature mechanism. However, the onset temperature of these reactions could be lower due to the instability of the electrodes as a result of the over-lithiation of the anode and delithiation of the cathode [11]. In this advanced state of overcharge, the cell voltage rises sharply to very high values because of a significant increase in resistance [24]. Consequently, the cell voltage may drop and be reduced to 0 V, as a result of ISCs. Finally, the occurrence of TR due to overcharging abuse leads to a more violent phenomenon, as the cell stores more energy to be released during the process, and a combination of several exothermic reactions takes place, involving the anode, cathode and electrolyte [26].

Having explained the overtemperature and overcharge mechanisms, this section introduces the proposed characterization methodology based on the identification of four abuse areas that complement the SOA in the abuse region. The concept of area is defined by the two dimensions in which the extent of each area is analyzed: temperature and SOC. The following section describes the phenomena and processes that occur in each area, as shown in Fig. 1, and presents various methods for determining the boundaries of each area.

SOA encompasses all those operating conditions within the safe limits established by the manufacturers in the datasheet. As mentioned above, the voltage limits are imposed by the 0 and 100% SOC, while the temperature range may depend on the operating state of the battery (stopped, charging or discharging) and current value. Under these conditions, in general, the battery operates safely, without the risk of harmful reactions. However, it is possible that even when working within the SOA, some undesired reactions may occur, such as lithium plating when charging with high currents at low temperatures [27].

Ultimately, no harmful reactions are considered to occur within the SOA and the cell operates in this area as long as its voltage and temperature conditions are within the safety ranges established by the manufacturer.

Once the cell exceeds the SOA limits, it enters the Accelerated Degradation Area (ADA). Thus, the boundary between SOA and ADA is defined by the maximum safety limits established by the battery manufacturer. It is true that there is a certain margin in which degradation reactions between the internal components of the battery do not yet occur, in order to ensure the safety of the battery in the event that these limits are slightly or briefly exceeded during operation. However, if the conditions are maintained, the cells may start to undergo accelerated degradation. This may affect their lifetime, reduce their capacity or lead to lithium plating.

The main characteristics of the behavior of the cell in this area are that it apparently works normally, without any anomaly being observed in its operation, such as abnormal heat generation or swelling. This could indicate the presence of gases in the interior. However, depending on the length of time the battery is kept in this zone, it is possible that irreversible reactions may occur that affect the performance and life of the battery. In the event of overtemperature, an inversely proportional relationship between internal resistance and temperature is observed within the ADA, which tends to decrease with increasing temperature and vice versa.

If the abuse conditions continue to move outside the safe ranges, then a series of unwanted reactions begin to occur between the internal components, which start to seriously compromise the safety of the battery. Therefore, the onset of the Gas Formation Area (GFA) is considered to occur at the same time as the onset of degradation reactions. As the name suggests, in this zone gases begin to form inside the cell as a result of the reactions taking place. As shown in Fig. 1, the first reactions that occur are the decomposition of the SEI layer and the vaporization of the electrolyte in the case of overtemperature, Fig. 1(a). With regard to abuse by overcharging, Fig. 1(b), the start of lithium plating begins, which reacts with the electrolyte and can produce micro ISCs.

Different criteria can be established to detect when this series of reactions starts and the cell moves from the ADA to the GFA. The clearest sign of the onset of reactions can be a sudden rise in temperature, due to the heat generated by the reactions. However, the heat released can be small and, with adequate heat dissipation by the cell, the temperature rise may be negligible. Therefore, other conditions need to be considered when defining the start of the GFA, such as the formation of gases inside the cell due to the reactions. These cause the internal pressure to increase and lead to the swelling of the cell. By measuring the internal pressure or deformation of the cell, the presence of gases can be detected, and ultimately the initiation of reactions that mark the boundary between ADA and GFA. However, since the measurement of internal pressure or cell deformation is not at all common in current systems, the onset of GFA will be considered to occur at the moment when the internal resistance changes from having an inversely proportional relationship with temperature to a directly proportional one. This change in the behavior of the internal resistance may be due to undesired reactions such as the thickening of the SEI layer due to the reaction between the lithium and the electrolyte, the loss of active material, or the formation of gases that separate the electrodes. This ultimately increases the resistance of the battery [28].

The increase in pressure inside the cell due to the gases produced by the different reactions leads to venting. The moment at which the gases are released into the atmosphere marks the beginning of the Gas Release Area (GRA). Once venting has occurred, toxic and flammable gases are continuously released into the atmosphere, posing a clear health risk to anyone nearby. In this area, the abuse of the cell is significant and is a clear warning that the cause of abuse must be avoided before it is too late and the TR phenomenon leading to cell self-destruction occurs.

The best way to detect venting is to have gas sensors that are able to alert to the presence of any gaseous substances released from a cell, such as CO
2
, CO, H
2
or volatile organic compounds (VOCs) [29]. The gas measurement equipment to be used does not need to be highly precise given that once venting occurs, it is not a matter of measuring the exact concentration of the gases released, but of detecting their presence due to a change in the composition of the ambient air. Consequently, it is not advisable to have equipment that measures the concentration of a single gas, as the measurement may not be as reliable as when using sensors that can detect a number of gases. Other ways to detect venting could be by measuring the decrease in cell temperature due to the Joule–Thomson effect. However, this temperature drop is very small or, in many cases, negligible. Having sensors to detect the temperature of the environment around the cells could be useful to detect the presence of gases, since these leave the cell at high temperatures and could therefore be measured. Finally, although some authors propose the measurement of the phase shift part of the cell impedance for the detection of venting [30], this involves having an online impedance measurement system, which is complex and requires high accuracy and sampling frequency.

Within the GFA, if the cause of abuse persists or even if it subsides or ceases, the state of abuse of the cell may be well advanced and there may come a time when a series of highly exothermic reactions are triggered, producing a sharp rise in cell temperature and causing TR. The TR lasts only a few seconds during which the temperature rate can reach 1000 °C/min, increasing the cell temperature by several hundred degrees Celsius. As a consequence of the violent phenomenon of the TR, the cell could explode and catch fire, which would take time to extinguish. TR is the most worrying phenomenon that can originate in a lithium-ion cell and then spread to all the neighboring cells in the battery.

The onset of the Thermal Runaway Area (TRA) is determined from the temperature rate, when it exceeds 10 °C/min. Other ways to detect the onset of TR may be the release of a large amount of gases, considerably greater than those released at the time of venting. In addition, a voltage drop to zero is a clear indicator of sudden cell death. However, this could happen moments before TR, as a result of an ISC, so this does not clearly correlate with the origin of TR.

In this work, a pouch type Li-ion/polymer cell is studied, model SLPB8643128H5, manufactured by Kokam, shown in Fig. 2. It has a nominal capacity of 3.6 Ah, a nominal voltage of 3.7 V and a maximum and minimum voltage for the charge–discharge protocol of 4.2 and 2.7 V respectively. This cell contains NMC-LCO composite cathode, the anode is composed of carbon/graphite and the electrolyte is based on a solution of lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiP
F
6
) in a mixture of organic solvent ethylene carbonate (EC) and ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC). The SOH of all cells tested is around 95 ± 1%. See other general specifications in Table 1.

In order to characterize the abuse region in LIBs, 13 tests were carried out in which safe operating conditions were exceeded up to TR. As indicated above, the cell is subjected to two types of abuse to study its behavior when overtemperature and overcharge occur. The conditions of all tests are shown in Table 2. As can be seen, in the 9 overtemperature tests the cell at different SOCs is heated from 25 °C up to TR, while in the 4 overcharge tests, the ambient temperature (
T
a m b
) varies in each test and the cell is charged from 0 until TR is triggered.

An initial characterization test was conducted to determine the real capacity of each cell. To do so, the cell was subjected to 3 charge/discharge cycles at C/3, keeping the ambient temperature constant at 25 °C. The CC–CV (constant current–constant voltage) mode is used for charging, with a maximum voltage of 4.2 V and tail current of C/20, and the CC mode was used for discharging with a minimum voltage of 2.7 V. The capacity discharged in the third discharge cycle was considered to be the actual cell capacity.

Before performing the abuse tests, the cells were charged under the same conditions as the capacity test up to their corresponding SOC, based on the calculated capacity. In the case of overtemperature tests with SOC above 100%, the overcharge from 100% was carried out by means of a constant current at C/3. During this pre-test overcharge, the cell swells up, as can be seen in Fig. 2, which is why the overcharge was carried out immediately prior to the test. As the cell temperature rises during the overcharge, the heating is expected to start when the cell temperature stabilizes with the ambient temperature. In the overcharge tests, the starting temperature was adjusted to the set conditions and the cell temperature was stabilized before starting the test.

Two objectives were sought when preparing the number and procedure of the overtemperature tests. The first was to perform a sweep of the overtemperature behavior at different SOCs, to compare the influence of SOC on the abuse areas limits. In overtemperature tests, the SOC is considered to remain constant to the initial SOC during the whole test. The second was to design a slow stepwise heating to allow sufficient time for the reactions inside the cell to take place at their onset temperatures, in particular to define the minimum ambient temperature capable of triggering the TR.

This test starts at 25 °C and consists of progressively increasing the ambient temperature in steps with a 90-minute duration. The set point temperatures for these steps are 60, 100, 120, 140, 155, 170 and 220 °C. The test is considered to be finished either when the cell is destroyed or when 60 min have elapsed in the last step of 220 °C and no exothermic behavior is observed in the cell. In the event that self-heating behavior of the cell is observed at any step that causes the cell temperature to be 5 °C higher or greater than that of the oven, then the temperature set point is maintained over time, beyond the corresponding 90-minute step, until the temperature difference drops below 3 °C. Fig. 3(a) shows the case for test 4, where the last temperature step at 155 °C was extended because the set conditions of exothermic behavior of the cell were met.

During the heating test, in order to measure the direct current internal resistance of the cell (
R
D C
), 10-second charge and discharge pulses at C/2 were performed every 5 min, as shown in Fig. 3(b). The
R
D C
can be calculated by Eq. (1), where
Δ V
is the change of the cell voltage during the pulse and Δ
I is the step current.
(1)
R
D C
=
Δ V
Δ I

Overcharge abuse tests are intended to characterize the behavior of the cell when a constant current is applied above SOCs higher than 100%. The SOC is estimated using the Coulomb counting method from the current introduced during the test and considering the actual capacity of the cell. In order to study the influence of temperature on this process, the tests are repeated at four different ambient temperatures.

In the overcharge test, the cell is charged with a constant current of 3C/4 until TR occurs. The cell starts from SOC = 0%, as shown in Fig. 3(c). The ambient temperature varies in each overcharge test, In order to measure the
R
D C
, in this test, interruptions in the charging current are made every 10 min, consisting in 10-second breaks in the cell charging. In this way, the
R
D C
can be recalculated from Eq. (1).

All the tests and measurements were carried out at the Laboratory for Energy Storage and Microgrids of the Public University of Navarre shown in Fig. 4. The capacity tests were conducted in an Ineletec INECC-30/150 climatic chamber at a controlled temperature of 25 °C, while the abuse tests were carried out in an 80 L resistant stainless-steel oven, which can heat the cell up to 220 °C. The charge and discharge processes were conducted with a Neware BTS4000 for SOCs under 100%, and a Xantrex XDC 300–20, a digital programmable DC power supply, for overcharge tests. The voltage and current measurements were captured every 100 ms by the cell tester itself and a YOKOGAWA WT3000 datalogger for SOCs above 100%. Five K type thermocouples measure the cell temperature, while three others measure the ambient temperatures at different points inside the oven. Cold-junction compensated thermocouple-to-digital converter, MAX31855, was used, with a reading accuracy of ±2 °C for temperatures ranging from –200 to 700 °C. Given that gases may be released in abuse tests on LIBs, the presence of gaseous substances generated by the cell was also measured, using a mobile gas-sensing unit by Dräger equipped with four sensors that measure O
2
, CO
2
, CO and hydrocarbons C
x
H
y
, with a sampling rate of 1 Hz. Four low-cost sensors specifically SGP30, SGP40, BME680 and SCD30, were also used, to detect volatile organic compounds (VOCs), H
2
y CO
2
. Both the Dräger equipment and the gas sensors were placed outside the oven, just above the exhaust vent. A Raspberry Pi 3B+ was used for the data acquisition of thermocouples and gas sensors, with a sampling rate of 15 and 2 Hz respectively.
In this section, the results of the 13 tests described in Section 4.2 are presented and analyzed. The behavior of the cell is studied independently for each type of abuse.

The temperatures limiting the boundary of the areas obtained from overtemperature abuse tests are presented in Table 3. The first two columns give the test identification number and the corresponding SOC of the cell. The next five columns show the temperatures that divide each abuse area and the maximum temperature reached during the abuse test. Finally, the last column records the ambient temperature when the cell reaches
T
0 , T R
(
T
0 , T R
a m b
).

Fig. 5 shows a more visual representation of the results presented in Table 3 to compare the relationships between SOC and abuse areas limiting temperatures. Starting with
T
s a f e , m a x
, 60 °C is considered from manufacturer specifications, because this is the maximum safe temperature at storage conditions. This temperature is only valid within a SOC between 0 and 100%.

Continuing with
T
d e g
, which separates ADA from GFA, it can be seen that the temperature is similar for all tests from 0 to 100% SOC. In addition, the value of
T
d e g
is in the range of the start of SEI decomposition, which could lead to an increase in
R
D C
and gas generation. Fig. 6 shows the
R
D C
values in per unit (in relation to the initial value at the lowest temperature) at different SOCs based on temperature. As can be seen,
R
D C
decreases with increasing temperature up to temperatures of 80–90 °C, where an inflection point occurs. This has mainly to do with the enhancement of the ionic conductivity of the electrolyte with increasing temperature. From this point onwards, the trend changes and
R
D C
increases exponentially with temperature, since a linear trend line can be observed in the graph and the y -axis is represented in logarithmic scale. In this case, the initiation of degradative reactions that consume active materials and produce gases are responsible for this significant increase in
R
D C
. As reported in [31], where LIB failure during high temperatures is studied in a cell with the same chemistry and manufacturer as in this paper, cell swelling was observed in the range from 90 to 100 °C, which corresponds to the inflection point in
R
D C
observed in the experiments. In the aforementioned study, the authors assumed that the cell swelling was mainly caused by the evaporation of the electrolyte components (EMC), given that the swelling disappeared upon cooling. In the case of the overcharged cells (tests with SOCs from 125 to 200%), it is not possible to determine a value for
T
d e g
during the overtemperature abuse, as degradation starts during the sample preparation to the desired SOC, as will be discussed in Section 5.2.

With regard to
T
v e n t
, there is a tendency to decrease with higher SOCs. In tests 8 and 9, with 175 and 200% SOC respectively, venting occurs beforehand during the overcharge. For 150% SOC test, it is observed that venting occurs at the first heating step of 60 °C, as the gas generation inside the cell may be considerable due to the overcharge conditions. However, the value of
T
v e n t
in test with 125% SOC differs from this trend and is, in fact, the highest of all the overtemperature tests. This could be explained by a delay or slowing down of the gas generation of the overtemperature side reactions. This is due to a loss of reactants during the previous overcharge reactions, such as the reaction of the lithium plating and the electrolyte, which consumes active material and thickens the SEI layer, which then has a suppressive effect on the reaction between the intercalated lithium in the anode and electrolyte.

TR was triggered in all tests except for the discharged cell (0% SOC), where neither the maximum ambient temperature nor the cell internal heat generation caused by side reactions, were sufficient to trigger TR and produce a sharp temperature increase. Moreover, the cell at 25% SOC has a high
T
0 , T R
and low
T
m a x
compared with higher SOCs. This reinforces the fact that lower SOCs make it more difficult for TR to occur and minimize its severity. It is important to note
T
0 , T R
a m b
, as it indicates the last heating step for the corresponding overtemperature test. The maximum oven temperature (220 °C) is required for the first two tests (0 and 25% SOC), the heating step of 170 °C for the third one (50% SOC) and, for the rest, the same heating step of 155 °C is able to trigger TR. Although the heating step is the same for SOCs from 75 to 200%, in Fig. 7 it can be seen that the time to trigger TR tends to be shorter for the higher SOCs. This demonstrates that at higher SOCs the side reactions are more significant and generate more heat in less time. Therefore, TRs at higher SOCs are more likely to occur and the consequences are more dangerous, with explosions occurring in tests with SOCs from 125 to 200%, causing the oven door to open in tests with 175 and 200%. As can be observed in Fig. 5,
T
m a x
shows a clear tendency to increase with SOC. However, it is difficult to compare one test with the other, as they may have been influenced by other factors. For example, the oven temperature during TR, which is higher in test 3 at 50% than in the subsequent tests, could explain why
T
m a x
in test 3 is higher than test 4 at 75%. Furthermore,
T
m a x
in test 5 at 100% is greater than in tests at 125 or 150%, which could be due to the consequences of the side reactions taking place during the previous overcharge, producing a loss of reactants that do not act in overtemperature reactions. However, the scope of this work focuses on defining the boundaries of each abuse zone, and
T
m a x
is not decisive here.

The overcharge results are presented in Table 4. The first two columns give the test identification number and the corresponding
T
a m b
during the test. The limit SOCs and temperatures dividing each abuse area are determined in the next columns. Due to the specifications of the investigated cell presented in Table 1,
T
s a f e , m a x
during charging is 45 °C. Due to this,
S O
C
s a f e , m a x
and
T
s a f e , m a x
conditions are only met in the test at
T
a m b
of 25 °C. In the other tests,
T
s a f e , m a x
is reached before the start of the experiment. Moreover, since the onset of TR is determined when the temperature rate reaches 10 °C/min, the cell destruction phenomenon is so fast that
S O
C
0 , T R
and
S O
C
m a x
are almost identical, so they will be considered to be equal. Finally, the last column gives the maximum voltage reached by the cell during overcharge.

Fig. 8 shows the results from Table 4 making it possible to observe the trends as a function of
T
a m b
. Looking at the limiting SOCs in the first instance, in Fig. 8(a) a clear trend of higher SOCs for lower
T
a m b
can be observed. This is true for
S O
C
v e n t
and
S O
C
0 , T R
(
S O
C
m a x
). These results are as expected, given that side reactions caused by overtemperature conditions are avoided at lower
T
a m b
, making overcharge reactions the main cause of heat generation and temperature increases. Once the cell temperature is higher, both overcharge and overtemperature conditions are responsible for triggering different side reactions. Until the cell reaches higher temperatures, more time is needed and consequently more energy is stored, resulting in higher SOCs for lower
T
a m b
. However,
S O
C
d e g
remains almost constant with increasing
T
a m b
, which suggests that the start of degradation reactions for this cell depends on overcharge conditions rather than temperature. For the highest
T
a m b
, the GFA starts during sample preparation as estimated in the overtemperature results, where
T
d e g
ranged from 83 to 90 °C. It is noteworthy that, although the
S O
C
m a x
of the cell at 25 °C is 178.79%, in the overtemperature tests the cell is able to pre-overcharge up to 200%. This is attributed to the charging current, which was set at C/3 for the sample preparation and 3C/4 for the overcharge test. Thus, the increase in the current value leads to increased heat generation and accelerated side reactions, lowering the onset temperature of TR. Therefore, the charging current can be a decisive factor in establishing the limiting SOCs and temperatures of the abuse areas.

In terms of the limiting temperatures, it can be observed in Fig. 8(b) that
T
d e g
is very similar to
T
a m b
, given that the abnormal heat generation does not start until the cell enters the GFA. Thus, heat generation within SOA and ADA is mainly due to the ohmic losses arising from the Joule effect. There is a considerable temperature increase in
T
v e n t
in comparison with
T
d e g
, since reactions are taking place inside the cell and generating heat and gases that contribute to the pressure rise until venting occurs. However, in the case of
T
a m b
=100 °C, venting occurs almost at the same temperature. This is due to the fact that, right from the beginning of the test, the cell is within GFA, as degradation started at around 80–90 °C. Therefore, gases are generated as a result of the high temperature from SOC=0% and, as can be observed,
S O
C
v e n t
is less than 100%. It is noteworthy that tests 12 and 13 at
T
a m b
of 75 and 100 °C respectively are special, as the overtemperature conditions are met right from the start of the test. The TR onset temperature is very similar in tests 10 and 11 with
T
a m b
of 25 and 50 °C, which could indicate that the same reactions are responsible for the sharp increase in temperature. However, it must not be forgotten that there is a considerable difference in
S O
C
0 , T R
and in
T
0 , T R
. Finally,
T
m a x
is again a clear sign of the severity of TR, whereby TR is more intense in the most overcharged cells, which correspond to those with lower
T
a m b
.

Fig. 9 shows the evolution of cell temperature with increasing SOC for all overcharge tests at different
T
a m b
. The cell temperature can be seen to remain almost constant when the cell is within SOA and ADA, with SOCs below
S O
C
d e g
. However, once the degradation reactions start, a sudden increase in temperature can be observed, which is followed by continuous heat generation. From the results obtained, a temperature plateau was observed minutes before the TR was triggered in tests 10–12 with
T
a m b
from 25 to 75 °C. This may suggest that at this point the heat generation of the side reactions is equal to the heat dissipation with the environment, and that it is the increase in SOC that triggers the final reactions that lead to TR. Further research is needed to determine the exact causes of this behavior. It is also important to note that tests 10 to 12 have a certain proportionality in terms of temperature evolution with the SOC and the onset of TR, despite the fact that the ambient temperature is different. Test 13 at 100 °C behaves differently from the rest, given that cell degradation occurs right form the start of the test, and the high ambient temperature accelerates the side reactions leading to TR, reaching a significantly lower
S O
C
m a x
.

Section 3 details various methods for obtaining the boundaries of the abuse areas, and Fig. 10 displays the monitored variables in the experiments conducted to determine these limiting temperatures and SOCs. Firstly,
T
s a f e , m a x
and
S O
C
s a f e , m a x
are obtained from the manufacturer’s datasheet, the main specifications of which are shown in Table 1. The boundary between ADA and GFA is identified differently in the case of overtemperature and overcharge tests. In the case of the overtemperature tests, no abnormal heat generation is detected, but a change in the trend of the
R
D C
is observed. This stopped decreasing with increasing temperature and started to increase, as can be seen in Fig. 10(a). The temperature at that inflection point determines
T
d e g
. In the overcharge tests, an abnormal heat generation from the cell is observed in the evolution of the temperature rate, resulting in a 10 °C increase of the temperature, as can be appreciated in Fig. 10(b). The uniqueness of this phenomenon has been thoroughly studied in a previous work and considered as the onset of degradation reactions due to overcharge [32]. Subsequently the
R
D C
begins to grow slightly, but it is this sudden increase in cell temperature that determines
T
d e g
and
S O
C
d e g
. The timing of venting is determined in all tests by information from the gas sensors used, providing a clear and unambiguous signal of gas release from the cell. In Fig. 10, the signal of the SGP30 gas sensor is shown, which measures the total volatile organic compounds (TVOC) concentration within a range of 0 to 60000 ppb. Finally, the onset of TRA in each test is determined from the ratio of the hottest temperature sensor when its rate reaches 10 °C/min. It is important to highlight the significance of selecting the hottest sensor as it provides a more reliable measurement of the actual cell surface temperature. During the abuse process, temperature sensors can separate or even detach from the cell’s interior due to swelling. A representation with a logarithmic scale on the y -axis has been used in Fig. 10(a) to show the heating provided by the oven at each temperature step.

With the results obtained in all tests, it is possible to determine the overall behavior of the cell in relation to its temperature and SOC conditions above the safe limits. With this aim in mind, the objective is to determine the limits of each abuse zone by selecting the temperature and SOC pair that divides each area. Since various temperature and overcharge conditions were analyzed in the tests, with overtemperature tests at different SOCs, and overcharge tests at different ambient temperatures, it has been possible to obtain variable results to indicate the limits of abuse areas. However, in order to define the boundaries of the various areas, the most restrictive conditions must be considered. Therefore, in the event of variable results, the SOC and temperature conditions with the lowest values were chosen, as they indicate the most conservative area change. Based on these considerations, Fig. 11 was prepared. The boundaries of the abuse areas are represented by the most restrictive values of limiting temperatures and SOCs obtained in the tests (marked with asterisks). This figure shows the characterization of the abuse region of the investigated cell under overtemperature and overcharge conditions, delimiting the different areas of abuse defined in this article. Thus, if the cell operating temperature and SOC conditions are known, more information is available on its state and the phenomena that may have occurred, based on the area in which it is located. This characterization into areas makes it possible to develop systems to detect abusive conditions in the cell, given that this article sets out the indicators that report the change of area and makes it possible to know the margin for the next change of area.

If we look at the boundaries obtained from the combination of cell behavior at elevated temperatures and SOCs, the following conclusions can be drawn. It is possible to observe a division within the SOA itself, since the manufacturer indicates a maximum storage temperature of up to 60 °C, but this is reduced to 45 °C if it is being charged. Therefore, between 45 and 60 °C it may come under SOA or ADA depending on the operation of the cell. In view of the results obtained, ADA maintains a temperature margin in relation to SOA for SOCs below 100% of about 25 °C in relation to 60 °C. This indicates that the degradation of the cell by overtemperature in this range of SOCs does not depend on the SOC, but only on the temperature. For SOCs greater than 100%, the ADA also presents a constant SOC margin of about 9% SOC. Therefore, in this case, temperature is not a critical factor for the onset of cell degradation, only its SOC. However, a slight trend to lower
S O
C
d e g
with increasing temperature can be seen. The boundary between GFA and GRA shows a similar constant temperature range for SOCs below 75%, with a limiting temperature of around 125 °C. Furthermore, unlike the boundary between ADA and GFA, for higher SOCs, the temperature can be seen to drop considerably as the SOC increases. This suggests that temperature significantly affects the overcharge mechanism, leading to a shorter GFA at higher temperatures. Thus, the lower the temperature, the more SOC is needed to reach GRA. Finally, a linear relationship between temperature and SOC can be seen when dividing GRA and TRA, which can be fitted with a trend line obtaining an equation of
T
0 , T R
= –0.79·
S O C
(%) + 246.5 (°C) with
R
2
= 0.971. Consequently, SOC becomes decisive in the onset of TR and can reduce or increase the onset temperature of this phenomenon.

Once this graph was obtained, the authors noted that the overcharge tests were more restrictive than the overtemperature tests on overcharged cells, so that overtemperature tests in overcharge conditions would not be essential in order to obtain the same abuse area limits. Despite this, these tests were of great interest as they have shown that previous overcharge does not affect the onset of TR, but does affect its severity. It has also been found that the value of the current applied during the overcharge could modify the TR mechanism, allowing a greater amount of energy to be stored before TR occurs.

This article proposes a new methodology for characterizing the abuse region of LIBs under overtemperature and overcharge conditions, based on the identification of four abuse areas. These abuse areas allow us to understand the behavior of LIBs in the abuse region and are applicable to any type of battery. Various methods have been highlighted to determine the boundaries of the areas, although it has been demonstrated that monitoring temperature, SOC, resistance and gas is sufficient to reliably establish the boundaries of each area. To demonstrate the usefulness of this methodology, a series of overtemperature tests at different SOCs and overcharge tests at different ambient temperatures have been conducted to characterize the abuse region as a function of cell temperature and SOC.

Although the method to achieve this characterization of abuse is expensive and time-consuming, it does provide valuable information for assessing the safety of a LIB based on its operating conditions. The work conducted in this study can help to standardize a testing, analysis and characterization methodology, based on the definition of the different areas of abuse, by the manufacturers. Given the wide variety of cells with different chemistries, sizes and shapes, this methodology will in turn help to improve the safe use and handling of batteries in their multiple applications. This information is extremely valuable for assessing the safety level of LIBs and the effectiveness of preventive measures. Moreover, it could also improve the classification of TR detection methods, in order to determine the area in which they can succeed in their warning mission.

Finally, this work opens a very interesting line of research with regard to the characterization of abuse areas in lithium-ion batteries, given that these may change over the useful life of the battery, depending on degradation and aging. The analysis of other parameters and techniques for the identification of the internal processes that take place in conditions of abuse in order to determine each area is another matter requiring further investigation.

Iñaki Lalinde: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Alberto Berrueta: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Joseba Arza: Writing review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Pablo Sanchis: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Alfredo Ursúa: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is part of the projects PID2019-111262RB-I00, funded by MCIN/AEI/10.13039/501100011033/, TED2021-132457B-I00, funded by MCIN/AEI/10.13039/501100011033/ and by the European Union “NextGenerationEU/PRTR”, STARDUST, funded by European Union’s Horizon 2020 research and innovation program, HYBPLANT, Spain (0011-1411-2022-000039), funded by Government of Navarre, Spain, and a Ph.D. scholarship (PRE2020-095314), funded by Spanish Ministry of Science and Innovation. Open access funding provided by Universidad Pública de Navarra, Spain .

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigations of the exothermic reactions of natural graphite anode for Li-ion batteries during thermal runaway
J Electrochem Soc

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Yang, H. Bang, K. Amine, and J. Prakash, “Investigations of the exothermic reactions of natural graphite anode for li-ion batteries during thermal runaway,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, vol. 152, pp. A73–A79, 01 2005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Overcharge-to-thermal-runaway behavior and safety assessment of commercial lithium-ion cells with different cathode materials: A comparison study
J Energy Chem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, J. Yuan, X. Zhu, H. Wang, L. Huang, Y. Wang, and S. Xu, “Overcharge-to-thermal-runaway behavior and safety assessment of commercial lithium-ion cells with different cathode materials: A comparison study,” Journal of Energy Chemistry, vol. 55, pp. 484–498, 2021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heat generation and thermal runaway mechanisms induced by overcharging of aged lithium-ion battery
Appl Therm Eng

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, Z. Wang, J. Bai, T. Gao, and N. Mao, “Heat generation and thermal runaway mechanisms induced by overcharging of aged lithium-ion battery,” Applied Thermal Engineering, vol. 212, p. 118565, 2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal behavior and failure mechanism of lithium ion cells during overcharge under adiabatic conditions
Appl Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Ye, H. Chen, Q. Wang, P. Huang, J. Sun, and S. Lo, “Thermal behavior and failure mechanism of lithium ion cells during overcharge under adiabatic conditions,” Applied Energy, vol. 182, pp. 464–474, 2016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium plating in a commercial lithium-ion battery – A low-temperature aging study
J Power Sources

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Petzl, M. Kasper, and M. A. Danzer, “Lithium plating in a commercial lithium-ion battery 2013 a low-temperature aging study,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 275, pp. 799–807, 2015.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Lalinde I, Berrueta A, Soto A, Arza J, Sanchis P, Ursúa A. Temperature Indicators and Overtemperature Detection in Lithium-Ion Batteries based on Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy. In: 2023 IEEE 32nd international symposium on industrial electronics. 2163-51452023, p. 1–6.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Lalinde, A. Berrueta, A. Soto, J. Arza, P. Sanchis, and A. Ursa, “Temperature indicators and overtemperature detection in lithium-ion batteries based on electrochemical impedance spectroscopy,” in 2023 IEEE 32nd International Symposium on Industrial Electronics (ISIE), 2023, pp. 1–6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of Li-ion battery failure and venting with Carbon Dioxide sensors
eTransportation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Cai, P. Valecha, V. Tran, B. Engle, A. Stefanopoulou, and J. Siegel, “Detection of li-ion battery failure and venting with carbon dioxide sensors,” eTransportation, vol. 7, p. 100100, 2021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid monitoring of impedance phase shifts in lithium-ion batteries for hazard prevention
J Power Sources

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Srinivasan, P. A. Demirev, and B. G. Carkhuff, “Rapid monitoring of impedance phase shifts in lithium-ion batteries for hazard prevention,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 405, pp. 30–36, 2018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preventing lithium ion battery failure during high temperatures by externally applied compression
J Energy Storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhao, Y. Patel, I. A. Hunt, K. M. Kareh, A. A. Holland, C. Korte, J. P. Dear, Y. B. Yue, and G. J. Offer, “Preventing lithium ion battery failure during high temperatures by externally applied compression,” Journal of energy storage, vol. 13, pp. 296–303, 2017.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Irujo E, Berrueta A, Lalinde I, Arza J, Sanchis P, Ursúa A. Onset of Irreversible Reactions in Overcharging Lithium-Ion Cells: an Experimental and Modeling Approach. In: 2023 IEEE 32nd international symposium on industrial electronics. 2163-51452023, p. 1–6.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Irujo, A. Berrueta, I. Lalinde, J. Arza, P. Sanchis, and A. Ursa, “Onset of irreversible reactions in overcharging lithium-ion cells: an experimental and modeling approach,” in 2023 IEEE 32nd International Symposium on Industrial Electronics (ISIE), 2023, pp. 1–6.
